title: Selecting Your Regularization
concept: Choose Ridge for dense features, Lasso for sparse feature selection, and ElasticNet for correlated datasets.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does ElasticNet combine in its penalty function?
exercises.explain: Exactly! ElasticNet is a hybrid that combines both L1 (absolute) and L2 (squared) weight penalties.

cards.title: The right tool
cards.body: A mechanic doesn't use a hammer for every job. Different engine problems require different tools. Similarly, different datasets require different regularization strategies.

cards.title: Regularization selection
cards.body: Selecting regularization is the process of analyzing feature properties (such as density, correlations, and dataset size) to choose Ridge, Lasso, or ElasticNet.

cards.title: The checklist
cards.body: Think of choosing a regularizer like sorting mail: keep all letters (Ridge), trash junk mail instantly (Lasso), or bundle similar bills and trash junk (ElasticNet).

cards.title: Practical guidelines
cards.body: Use Ridge if you have fewer features than samples and expect all features to matter. Use Lasso if you have thousands of features and want a simple model. Use ElasticNet if you have highly correlated features.

cards.title: No single default
cards.body: A common myth is that Ridge is always the safe default. While it's popular, if your dataset has redundant or noisy features, starting with ElasticNet often yields a better and more robust model.

cards.title: Regularization Decision Matrix
cards.body: Quick guide to choosing your regularization technique:

left.text:
Use Ridge if all features have small effects.
Use Lasso if you want to eliminate useless features.

right.text:
Use ElasticNet if features are correlated.
Or if you want a balance of L1 and L2.

exercises.prompt: When is Ridge Regression (L2) usually preferred over Lasso Regression (L1)?

options.text: When most features in the dataset are somewhat useful
When we want to set 90% of the weights to exactly zero
When we only have one training sample

exercises.explain: Ridge keeps all features, making it ideal when most features carry some predictive power.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If you have thousands of features and suspect only a few are actually important, Lasso is a good choice.
exercises.explain: True! Lasso will automatically zero out the useless features, leaving a clean, simple model.

exercises.sentence: For datasets with highly correlated features, we should generally choose ___ over Lasso.
exercises.explain: ElasticNet handles correlated features much better than Lasso by grouping them instead of selecting randomly.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following scenarios is the best fit for Lasso Regression?

options.text: A genetic dataset with 10,000 genes where only 5 are suspected of causing a disease
A house-pricing dataset with 3 highly correlated size measurements
A dataset with 5 features where every single feature is critical

exercises.explain: Lasso is perfect when you need to select a tiny subset of active features from a massive pool of candidates.

exercises.prompt: You are building a model to predict employee performance using 50 features. You find that several features are correlated (like years of experience, age, and salary), and you also suspect many features are completely useless. What is the most robust choice?

options.text: ElasticNet, because it handles the correlated features while still performing feature selection.
Ridge, because it will delete the useless features.
Lasso, because it will keep all the correlated features and share weights.

exercises.explain: Perfect! ElasticNet handles the correlation groups smoothly and still zeroes out the completely useless features, making it the most robust choice. Congratulations on mastering regularization! You're ready to protect your models from overfitting like a pro!
